content.full_text:
State of California 
Memorandum 
To 
From 
Commissioners Houston, McAndrews 
and Metzger 
/1 / 
FAIRI POJ.I'ICb.L PRACTICES COMMISSION 
B/r~l1.:(. tr~/t.~~ 
Dote 
Jan. 20, 1981 
I 
Subject: Advice to Adriana Gianturco 
This letter is the result of the first of a series 
of discussions we are having with agency heads concerning 
possible conflicts of interest revealed in the reports 
prepared by technical assistance. 
I thought you might 
like to see this letter before it is sent in case Adriana 
Gianturco or Cal Trans mentions it publicly. 
We plan to 
mail the letter on Thursday afternoon. 
BArvt:plh 
Attachment 


I 
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Technical Assistance 
(916) 322·5662 
Administratian 
322·5660 
Adriana Gianturco, Director 
Department of Transportation 
1120 N Street 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
Dear Ms. Gianturco: 
Executive/Legal 
322·5901 
Enfarcement 
322-6441 
January 22, 1981 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
As a result of our discussions, you have requested 
written advice as to whether you may participate in Department 
decisions which may affect Union Pacific, in view of the 
fact that your husband has a beneficial interest of over 
$10,000 of Union Pacific stock. 
In particular, you asked 
whether you could make decisions or participate in decisions 
involving: 
(1) 
Negotiations among Cal Trans, Union Pacific 
and AMTRAK for passenger rail service in California; 
(2) 
A decision by Cal Trans concerning participation 
in proceedings before the Interstate Commerce Commission 
(ICC) regarding a proposed merger involving Union Pacific; 
(3) 
Decisions relating to purchases of land which 
include parcels owned by Union Pacific. 
Under the provisions of the Cal Trans' Conflict of f9terest 
Code, and Government Code Sectior.s 87100 and 87103,-
you 
(1) must disqualify yourself from the negotiations with 
Union Pacific and AMTRAK, (2) should disqualify yourself 
from decisions regarding the ICC proceeding, and (3) may 
have to disqualify yourself from decisions involving purchases 
of land, depending on the circumstances. 
Both the Cal Trans Conflict of Interest Code and Section 
87100 prohibit a public official from making, or participating 
in, decisions in which the official knows or has reason to 
!/ All statutory references are to the Government Code 
unless otherwise noted. 


Adriana Gianturco, Director 
January 22, 1981 
Page Two 
know that he or she has a financial interest. 
You are considered 
to have a financial interest in a decision if that decision 
will have a "material financial effect" on any business 
entity in which you have a direct or indirect investment 
worth more than $1,000. 
Section 87103(b). 
An indirect 
investment includes, among other things, an investment owned 
by your spouse. 
Section 87103. 
Therefore you must disqualify 
yourself from participation in any decisions ~9ich may have 
a material financial effect on Union Pacific.-
"Material 
financial effect" has been defined by the Fair Political 
Practices Commission in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702. 
As 
a general rule, a decision will have a material financial 
effect on Union Pacific if it is reasonably foreseeable that 
the decision will increase or decrease Union Pacific's annual 
gross income by $100,000 or its annual net income by $50,000, 
or the value of its assets or liabilities by $100,000. 
Applying these standards to your situation at Cal Trans, 
you must disqualify yourself from all decision-making or 
participation in negotiations involving Cal Trans, A.viTRAK 
and Union Pacific for passenger rail service, since a decision 
to provide such service over Union Pacific tracks would 
clearly have a material financial effect on Union Pacific. 
The fact that Cal Trans will not contract directly with 
Union Pacific does not chanae this, because it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the terms of a contract between Cal Trans 
and AHTRAK will have a material financial effect on any 
related contract between Union Pacific and AMTRAK. 
Turning to your second question, you should disqualify 
yourself in the case of the ICC proceedings regarding a 
proposed merger involving Union Pacific. 
It is clearly 
foreseeable that the outcome of the merger application may 
have a material financial effect on Union Pacific. 
I assume 
that Cal Trans will intervene in the proceedings only if it 
believes such intervention will have a significant effect on 
whether the merger takes place. 
It is also possible that 
Cal Trans' intervention will have a @aterial financial effect 
on the legal fees and other costs associated with Union 
Pacific's merger application. 
In addition, the value of 
your husband's stock in the corporation may be directly 
affected by the merger. 
Under these circumstances you should 
disqualify yourself from participating in any of the Department's 
~/ This would not be the case if the Union Pacific 
stock is owned by a common trust fund as defined in Section 
82034. 
.. 


Adriana Gianturco, Director 
January 22, 1981 
Page Three 
deliberations concerning whether Cal Trans should intervene 
in the ICC proceeding, and, if so, whether it should support 
or oppose the merger. 
In answer to your third question, you must disqualify 
yourself from decisions involving purchases of land which 
include parcels owned by Union Pacific if you know or have 
reason to know that the purchase involves $100,000 worth of 
Union Pacific property, or meets any of the other criteria 
for materiality set forth in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702. 
As you descr~ed Cal Trans' procedures, you are not normally 
informed of the identity of the owners of parcels of land 
included in a proposed land purchase. 
Unless you actually 
know Union Pacific land is involved, or the circumstances 
are such that you have reason to know such land is involved 
(e.g., the purchase is along Union Pacific rail lines), you 
need not disqualify yourself. 
You do not need to change 
your customary procedures in order to inquire specifically 
about the ownership of every parcel of land included in 
every proposed purchase by Cal Trans to determine whether or 
not Union Pacific owns any parcels. 
However, you should 
inquire if you have reason to know that Union Pacific land 
may be included in the purchase. 
You also asked what responsibility you have to keep 
track of changes in your husband's investments. 
Again, the 
question is whether you know or have reason to know of a 
financial interest. 
Whenever your husband is informed of a 
change in his trust investments by his trustee, you would be 
considered to have reason to know of the change. 
If he is 
not informed of a change in the trust portfolio as a result 
of a reasonable and customary policy on behalf of the trustee, 
you have no affirmative obligation to seek the information 
yourself. 
Finally, you inquired as to whether you have to report 
your husband's income. 
If your husband's incrnne is not 
community property (he lives in Massachusetts, the marriaye 
occurred in Massachusetts, and it is agreed that his income 
is his separate property) you would ordinarily have no reporting 
obligation with respect to that income. 
The only circumstance 
under which you might have such a reporting obligation would 
be if he had a 10 percent or greater investment interest in 
the law firm from which he draws his income. 
Section 82030. 


Adriana Gianturco, Director 
January 22, 1981 
Page Four 
I hope this letter clarifies .your responsibilities with 
regard to the disclosure and disqualification provisions of 
the Political Reform Act. I will be happy to answer have any 
future questions. 
Very truly yours, 
~

Barbara A. Milman

Staff Counsel 
v 
Legal Division 
BAM:plh 


445-2201 
TRANSPORTATION 
November 25, 1980 
MS. BARBARA A. MILMAN 
Counsel, Legal Division 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
P. O. Box 
807 
Sacramento, CA 
95804 
Dear Ms. Milman: 
EDMUND G. BROWN JR., Governor 
You have inquired as to the disclosure on Schedule 2 of 
Union Pacific stock. 
On Schedule 2, I have disclosed my husband's stock interests. 
I have no interest in the Union Pacific stock or any of the 
other disclosed securities shown on Schedule 2. 
You also inquired of contacts by the Department with 
Amtrak on Union Pacific lines. 
Enclosed is a map showing 
all Union Pacific service within California. 
Note that 
at positions U-5 and U-6 there is shown in blue a Union 
Pacific line. 
We are currently discussing with Am~rak 
the possibility of operating a commuter service on this 
line. 
I would be glad to meet with you to discuss Amtrak con-
tacts or any other question regarding my disclosure statement. 
Sincerely, 
ADRIANA GIANTURCO 
Director of Transportation 
Enclosure 


STATE OF CALIFORNIA-BUSINESS AND TRANSPORTATION AGENCY 
EDMUND G. BROWN JR., Governor 
DEPARTMENT OF TRANSPORTATION 
OFFICE OF DIRECTOR 
1120 N STREET 
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 
(916) 445-2201 
December 10, 1980 
Ms. Barbara A. Milman 
Counsel, Legal Division 
Fair Politcal Practices Commission 
P. O. Box 807 
Sacramento, CA 
95804 
Dear Ms. Milman: 
I have some further thoughts on the Union Pacific matter and 
would like to receive your advice. 
Until the Union Pacific issue was brought to my attention by 
our Legal Division following your contact with them, I was 
unaware there even was an issue. 
The Union Pacific stock in 
question was (as of early last spring) part of the investment 
portfolio of a trust fund established in 1932 for my husband, 
John L. Saltonstall, Jr., by his father, John L. Saltonstall. 
I receive no benefits from this trust fund and have no in-
fluence over its management. 
Although I've never checked 
into this, I assume my husband also is not involved in the 
management of the fund's portfolio. 
For all I know, the stock 
may no longer even be part of the portfolio. 
If you believe there is a conflict of interest or an appearance 
of a conflict of interest in my having anything to do with 
matters potentially relating to Union Pacific, even through 
third parties (i.e., AMTRAK), I would like you to so advise 
me within the next month. 
Awaiting your opinion, I plan to 
separate myself from any decision-making involving Union Pacific. 
I look forward to sitting down with you and discussing this 
whole matter on Monday, December 15. 
Sincerely, 
ADRIANA GIANTURCO 
Director of Transportation 
cc: 
D. Rypinski 
R. Herbold 


:)tate 'of California 
Business and Transportation Agency 
Memorandum 
To 
Ron Herbold 
Deputy Director 
Planning and Programming 
Date 
: December 10, 1980 
File No.: 
From 
: DEPARTMENT OF TRANSPORTATION 
Director's Office 
Subject: 
Attached to the FPPC 
A trust fund established for my husband owned (as of last spring) 
stock in Union Pacific. 
For the next month, I would like you to handle any matter that 
normally would be brought to me and that might potentially affect, 
directly or indirectly, Union Pacific. 
fl £'utut'U] ;!!;cL&.~tJ.L-1{~ 
ADRIANA GIANTURCO 
Director of Transportation 
cc: 
DRypinski 
Barbara Milman, FPPC 
Attachment

sections.conclusion: Under the Cal Trans Conflict of Interest Code and Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103: (1) you must disqualify yourself from the negotiations with Union Pacific and AMTRAK, (2) should disqualify yourself from decisions regarding the ICC proceeding, and (3) may have to disqualify yourself from decisions involving purchases of land, depending on the circumstances. You must disqualify yourself from any decisions which may have a material financial effect on Union Pacific, defined generally as decisions that would increase or decrease Union Pacific's annual gross income by $100,000, net income by $50,000, or asset/liability value by $100,000.
sections.question_synthetic: Ms. Gianturco, Director of the Department of Transportation, requested advice on whether she must disqualify herself from various departmental decisions that could affect Union Pacific, given that her husband owns over $10,000 in Union Pacific stock. The specific matters involved AMTRAK negotiations, ICC merger proceedings, and land purchase decisions.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Ms. Gianturco must disqualify herself from AMTRAK/Union Pacific negotiations and should disqualify herself from ICC merger proceedings because these decisions would foreseeably have material financial effects on Union Pacific. Regarding land purchases, she need only disqualify herself if she knows or reasonably should know that Union Pacific property is involved and the purchase meets materiality thresholds, without requiring affirmative investigation of every parcel.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Complete advice letter with full question and conclusion sections. Document includes preliminary memorandum and supporting correspondence from Gianturco to FPPC and internal memos. OCR quality is good with only minor scanning artifacts. All substantive content extracted accurately.

change in his trust investments by his trustee, you would be 
considered to have reason to know of the change. 
If he is

Adriana Gianturco, Director

I hope this letter clarifies .your responsibilities with 
regard to the disclosure and disqualification provisions of 
the Political Reform Act. I will be happy to answer have any 
future questions.

CONCLUSION: Under the Cal Trans Conflict of Interest Code and Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103: (1) you must disqualify yourself from the negotiations with Union Pacific and AMTRAK, (2) should disqualify yourself from decisions regarding the ICC proceeding, and (3) may have to disqualify yourself from decisions involving purchases of land, depending on the circumstances. You must disqualify yourself from any decisions which may have a material financial effect on Union Pacific, defined generally as decisions that would increase or decrease Union Pacific's annual gross income by $100,000, net income by $50,000, or asset/liability value by $100,000.

embedding.summary: This FPPC advice letter to Adriana Gianturco, Director of Transportation, addresses conflict of interest disqualification requirements relating to her husband's Union Pacific stock holdings across multiple categories of departmental decisions. The letter provides detailed guidance on materiality standards and the circumstances under which disqualification is required or recommended.
